name: Amr ibn al-As
epithet: The Mind That Turned to Faith
title: Amr ibn al-As (RA): The Mind That Turned to Faith
meta_description: The story of Amr ibn al-As (RA), the shrewdest politician of Quraysh who once schemed against the Prophet Muhammad and later turned all of that intelligence toward Islam.
keywords: Amr ibn al-As, story of Amr ibn al-As, Najashi, conquest of Egypt, Rafah, sahaba stories, the firsts
lede: He was the most well-travelled man in Makkah and, by reputation, the most cunning. For years he used every bit of that intelligence to plot against the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, in one battle after another. People said no one could escape his schemes.

This is the story of Amr ibn al-As (may Allah be pleased with him): the politician of the Arabs, who spent the first part of his life fighting the truth, and the rest of it carrying it across the world.

sections.h2: The oldest of the young, the youngest of the old
paras: Amr ibn al-As was a poet, a horseman, and a merchant of Quraysh. Imam al-Dhahabi records that he was called the most cunning of the Arabs, the one men measured themselves against in sharpness of mind. He combined nearly every quality his people prized.

He was older than Umar ibn al-Khattab and Khalid ibn al-Walid, but younger than the elders who first declared war on the Prophet ﷺ. That placed him between two generations: old enough to sit with the chiefs, young enough to stand with their sons. He used to say he remembered the night Umar was born.

Allah was also preparing him in a way no one could see. As a trader he had crossed into Syria, Egypt, Abyssinia, Yemen, and beyond, again and again. He knew their roads, their cities, and their leaders. Decades before he would return to those lands as a Muslim, he already knew the ground beneath his feet.

He was known for his generosity, and he inherited his father's entire fortune. Later, as a Muslim, the Prophet ﷺ would watch him give that wealth away in charity, and say of him that he had much good with Allah. The man of the world was slowly being turned toward the next.

sections.h2: The man sent to break the refugees
paras: When a group of early Muslims fled the persecution of Makkah and took refuge in Abyssinia, Quraysh chose Amr to bring them back. Two of his own brothers were among those who had fled, yet he was the one most driven to drag them home in chains.

He knew the Christian king, the Najashi, from years of trade. So he came with a plan. The night before his audience, he visited each of the king's generals and bought their loyalty with gifts. The next day he told the Najashi these were simply troublemakers who had fled their own people, not worth a hearing.

The Najashi refused to act on one side of a story. "What kind of a man would I be if I did not hear their case?" he said. As the scholars note, this is what justice is: hearing both sides, and not being driven by bias.

He called the Muslims forward. Ja'far ibn Abi Talib (may Allah be pleased with him) described their faith plainly: a people who had worshipped idols and broken their bonds, until God sent them a prophet who commanded truth, kept promises, kindness to family, and protection of the weak. The king listened, looked at Amr, and said he saw nothing that required him to hand these people over. Amr's bribes and his cleverness had failed completely.

Amr returned to Makkah on the first failed mission of his life, humiliated. He took his anger out on the Muslims who remained, his own brother Hisham among them. He fought the Prophet ﷺ at Badr and Uhud, and he was one of the chief planners behind the siege of Madinah.

At last the Prophet ﷺ raised his hands and prayed against Amr. He had prayed against others, and they had been ruined. But this time something different happened: Allah sent down a verse in reply, telling His Prophet that this man's fate was not his to seal.
It was a quiet act of mercy hidden inside a moment of war. The door to Amr was not being shut. It was being held open, by Allah Himself, for a day no one could yet imagine.

His brother Hisham, tortured for his faith, had once despaired of ever being forgiven for giving in. A verse of the Quran came to lift that despair too: that no one should ever lose hope in the mercy of Allah. This was a family Allah refused to give up on.

ayah.text: Whether God relents towards them or punishes them is not for you [Prophet] to decide: they are wrongdoers.

paras: After the siege of Madinah failed, Amr read the signs and concluded the Prophet ﷺ would win. So he gathered some friends and proposed they return to Abyssinia and seek refuge with the Najashi, the way the Muslims once had.

There, in the king's court, Amr found a Muslim envoy from Madinah and asked permission to kill him. The Najashi struck him so hard he nearly broke his nose, then said the words that changed everything: "You ask me to hand over the messenger of the man to whom the same angel comes that came to Moses." Amr later said that in that instant, faith entered his heart.

He asked the Najashi to take his pledge to Islam right there, and so the king who had never met the Prophet ﷺ gave the testimony of faith to a future companion. Amr slipped away from his old friends in the night and rode straight for Madinah. On the road he met Khalid ibn al-Walid, bound for the same destination, for the same reason.
Together they reached the Prophet ﷺ among the last of those who would ever migrate to him. The sons of the men who had fought him hardest were now arriving not to attack, but to embrace him.

sections.h2: Islam wipes away what came before
paras: When Amr reached the Prophet ﷺ, he held back his hand. He would give his pledge, he said, only on the condition that all his past sins be forgiven. The Prophet ﷺ told him gently: "Do you not know that Islam wipes away whatever came before it?"

Notice what the Prophet ﷺ did not say. He did not invent a special pardon to win a useful convert. He pointed to a mercy already built into the faith, open to anyone who enters it. Amr's record was long and dark, and all of it was lifted at once.

Soon after, the Prophet ﷺ said of him in one narration, "People became Muslim, and Amr ibn al-As truly believed," testifying that faith had genuinely settled in his heart. The most hated man had become one of the trusted few.

And he was eager to prove it. One night an alarm sounded at the edge of Madinah, and Amr grabbed his sword and rushed to guard the city. The Prophet ﷺ pointed to him and asked the others, "Would you not have done what these two believers did?" Amr would later say he loved the Prophet ﷺ so deeply that he could barely lift his eyes to look at him.

Amr now turned his whole mind toward the faith he had fought. In the years after the Prophet ﷺ, he led armies into the lands he had once crossed as a merchant. Much of southern Palestine was opened under his command, clearing the way for Jerusalem to be received in peace.

Then he set his sights on Egypt. Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him) was hesitant, but Amr had a plan. He stationed himself at Rafah, in Gaza, on the very border, and from there his letters travelled back and forth to Madinah. He told the Egyptians the Prophet ﷺ had instructed the Muslims to treat them with kindness, for they were kin through Hajar, the mother of Ismail.

With only four thousand men he brought Egypt into Islam, and many of its people embraced the faith because of how fairly they were treated. He built a new city of tents called Fustat, the heart of what would later become old Cairo, and the mosque he founded there still carries his name today.

Umar said of him that it did not suit Amr ibn al-As to walk the earth as anything but a leader. The cunning Quraysh had once feared was now spent in service, and the man they thought too slippery to trust had become one of Islam's great commanders.

Amr ibn al-As died past the age of ninety. As recorded in Sahih Muslim, his son Abdullah sat with him at the end and saw his father weighed down by regret. So Amr divided his life into three phases for him.

In the first, he said, he fought the Prophet ﷺ, and had he died then he would surely have been among the people of the Fire. In the second, he embraced Islam and stood beside the Prophet ﷺ, and had he died then he was certain of Paradise. But the third phase, after the Prophet ﷺ, with all its trials and its mistakes, he could not be sure of himself.

His son tried to comfort him, reminding him how the Prophet ﷺ had loved him and trusted him with great affairs. Amr would not lean on it. He could not be certain, he said, whether that nearness was love or simply the Prophet ﷺ softening a hard heart. So he chose to lean on Allah alone.

Then he raised his finger to the sky. "O Allah, You commanded us and we left much of it. You forbade us and we fell into it. There is no god but You." He kept repeating those words, there is no god but Allah, until he passed away upon them.

lessons_heading: What the life of Amr ibn al-As teaches us
lessons_intro: His is the story of a man whose gifts never changed, only the direction he pointed them. That turn is the whole lesson.

lessons.text: The same cunning he once used to plot against the truth later carried Islam across continents. Talent is not the problem; what we aim it at is.

lessons.bold: The door is held open longer than we think.
lessons.text: Allah revealed a verse to keep Amr's fate undecided at the height of his enmity. No one is so far gone that mercy gives up on them.
He feared his long record could never be forgiven. He was told plainly that Islam wipes away whatever came before it.

lessons.bold: Honest fear of God is a mercy.
lessons.text: At ninety, he still examined his own heart rather than rest on his rank. That humility kept him reaching for Allah to his last breath.

faq.a: A poet, merchant, and famed strategist of Quraysh, known as the most cunning of the Arabs. He opposed the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ for years, then accepted Islam and became a major commander and the leader who brought Egypt into Islam.

faq.q: How did Amr ibn al-As become Muslim?
faq.a: He travelled to Abyssinia and met its Christian king, the Najashi, who told him plainly that Muhammad ﷺ was a true prophet and advised him to follow him. Amr said that in that moment faith entered his heart, and he took his pledge to Islam through the Najashi before riding to Madinah.

faq.q: What is Amr ibn al-As known for?
faq.a: He opened much of southern Palestine and then led the conquest of Egypt with only four thousand men, stationing himself at Rafah in Gaza. He founded the city of Fustat near present-day old Cairo, where the mosque named after him still stands.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Amr ibn al-As?
faq.a: That gifts can be turned from harm to good, that Allah leaves the door of mercy open even to His opponents, that entering Islam clears what came before, and that honest fear of God keeps the heart humble to the end.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It is tempting to believe some people are simply written off: too clever, too proud, too far down the wrong road to ever turn around. Amr's life refuses that idea. At the very moment he was scheming hardest against the truth, Allah was quietly keeping his door open. The same mind he used to harm became the mind that carried a mercy to whole nations.

Most of us are not changing the map of the world. But each of us has gifts, a sharp tongue, a quick mind, a strong will, that can be pointed either way. His story asks a gentle question of our ordinary day: what am I aiming my best at? And it leaves us with the humility he died on, never sure of ourselves, always reaching for Allah, repeating that there is no god but Him.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Amr ibn al-As (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 3:128). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

For years he used every bit of it to plot against the Prophet ﷺ.

Then everything turned.

Amr ibn al-As had crossed Syria, Egypt, Abyssinia, and Yemen as a merchant.

He knew their roads and rulers, long before he would return to those lands as a Muslim.

Quraysh sent him to drag the Muslim refugees out of Abyssinia.

Two of his own brothers were among them. He still wanted them back in chains.

The Prophet ﷺ once raised his hands to pray against Amr.

Instead, Allah sent down a verse: whether He forgives them or punishes them is not for you to decide.

The door was being held open.

Years later, in Abyssinia, the Najashi told him plainly:

"You ask me to hand over the messenger of a true prophet."

Amr said faith entered his heart in that instant.

He reached the Prophet ﷺ and asked that all his past sins be forgiven first.

"Do you not know," the Prophet ﷺ said, "that Islam wipes away whatever came before it?"

With four thousand men he brought Egypt into Islam.

He camped at Rafah, in Gaza, and treated the people with a fairness many had never known.

**Your strengths can be redeemed.**

The cunning he aimed at the truth later carried it across the world.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
He was the shrewdest politician of Quraysh, and for years he pointed all of it against the Prophet ﷺ. Amr ibn al-As (may Allah be pleased with him) fought at Badr and Uhud and helped plan the siege of Madinah.

Then, in the court of a Christian king in Abyssinia, he was told plainly that Muhammad ﷺ was a true prophet. He said faith entered his heart in that moment. He rode to Madinah, accepted Islam, and was told that entering the faith wipes away everything before it.

The same mind he once used to harm later carried Islam from Rafah into Egypt.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The most cunning man in Makkah, and the verse that saved him
email.alt_subject: "Islam wipes away whatever came before it"
email.preview: He spent years plotting against the Prophet ﷺ. Then a Christian king told him the truth.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

They called him the most cunning of the Arabs. Amr ibn al-As (may Allah be pleased with him) was a poet, a merchant, and the sharpest strategist of Quraysh. For years he aimed all of it at the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. He fought him at Badr and Uhud. He helped plan the siege of Madinah.

At one point the Prophet ﷺ raised his hands to pray against him. He had prayed against others, and they were ruined. But this time Allah sent down a verse in reply, telling His Prophet that this man's fate was not his to decide. The door to Amr was not being shut. It was being held open.

The change came in an unlikely place. Amr travelled to Abyssinia and met its Christian king, a man he knew well from years of trade. The king told him plainly that Muhammad ﷺ was a true prophet, the same kind of messenger who came to Moses, and advised him to follow him. Amr said that in that instant, faith entered his heart.

He rode to Madinah and asked the Prophet ﷺ that all his past sins be forgiven before he gave his pledge. The Prophet ﷺ did not invent a special pardon for him. He simply said: "Do you not know that Islam wipes away whatever came before it?" A long, dark record, lifted at once.

And he was eager to live up to it. When an alarm sounded at the edge of Madinah one night, he was among the first to grab his sword and rush to guard the city. He would later say he loved the Prophet ﷺ so deeply he could barely raise his eyes to look at him.

The rest of his life pointed the other way. The same mind he once used to harm carried Islam from Rafah, in Gaza, into Egypt, where many embraced the faith because of how fairly they were treated.

There is a quiet lesson in him that is easy to miss. Our gifts are not the problem. What we aim them at is. And the door of mercy stays open far longer than we tend to believe.

May Allah be pleased with him, and turn the best of us toward the best of things.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: He was the most well-travelled man in Makkah and, by reputation, the most cunning. For years Amr ibn al-As (may Allah be pleased with him) used every bit of that intelligence to plot against the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. People said no one could escape his schemes.

When a group of early Muslims fled to Abyssinia, Quraysh sent Amr to bring them back. Two of his own brothers were among them, yet he was the most determined to drag them home. He bribed the Christian king's generals and asked the king to hand the refugees over. The king refused to act on one side of a story, and let them stay.

Amr came home humiliated, and grew only angrier. He fought the Prophet ﷺ at Badr and Uhud and helped plan the siege of Madinah. At last the Prophet ﷺ prayed against him. But Allah sent down a verse in reply, telling His Prophet that Amr's fate was not his to seal. The door was being held open.

When the siege failed, Amr returned once more to Abyssinia. There the king struck him for his arrogance, then told him the truth: "You ask me to hand over the messenger of the man to whom the same angel comes that came to Moses." Amr said that in that instant faith entered his heart, and he took his pledge to Islam through the king who had never met the Prophet ﷺ.

He rode to Madinah and held back his hand, asking that all his past sins be forgiven first. The Prophet ﷺ told him gently, "Do you not know that Islam wipes away whatever came before it?" His long record was lifted at once, and the most hated man became one of the trusted few.

Now he turned his whole mind toward the faith he had fought. With only four thousand men he opened Egypt, camping at Rafah in Gaza, and treated its people with a fairness many had never known. The mosque he founded near old Cairo still carries his name.

He died past the age of ninety, weighing his life in three phases for his son, never quite sure of himself in the end. He raised his finger to the sky and kept repeating that there is no god but Allah, until those words were the last he spoke.
storybook.pullquote: Do you not know that Islam wipes away whatever came before it?
storybook.reflection: Amr's gifts never changed; only the direction he aimed them did. The same sharp mind he once used to harm later carried a mercy across the world. His life is a quiet promise that no one is written off, and a gentle question for our own day: what am I pointing my best at?
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
